predictions.source: I te po e tahi, ni oko atu he toa e igoa kia Tefolaha ki Nanumea. Tefolaha ni taua tele i taua e uke i te Pasefika. Ni fiu a ia i te taua tele tela ni manako a ia o fakafou tona olaaga. Teelaa ni faimalaga iai a ia i te moana i po e uke oko atu iai ki Nanumea.
predictions.reference: One day, a warrior named Tefolaha arrived in Nanumea. Tefolaha had fought in battles all over the Pacific. But he was tired of fighting and wanted to make a new life. He travelled on the ocean for many days before landing on Nanumea.
predictions.prediction: One night, a warrior named Tefolaha arrived at Nanumea. Tefolaha had fought in many battles across the Pacific. He had grown weary of all those battles and he thought about renewing his life. That is why he made a journey across the ocean for many nights until he arrived at Nanumea.

predictions.source: Ni lavea e Tefolaha a kalofaga i te one. Ni tautali e ia a kalofa kola kae ni oko atu ki he fale faka Tuvalu, teelaa e ‘noho mai iai a Pai mo Vau e ‘laga laa kete kae `fili a laa fau. Ni a’lofa laua kiai tela tuku e laua kiai mea’kai mo meainu mo he fau ki tona piho.
predictions.reference: Tefolaha saw footprints in the sand. They followed them to a Tuvaluan house, where Pai and Vau were weaving baskets and garlands. They felt sorry for him and gave him food and drink and put a garland on his head.
predictions.prediction: Tefolaha found some coconuts on the beach. He followed those coconuts and arrived at a Tuvaluan house, where Pai and Vau were sitting weaving baskets and selecting their pandanus leaves. They were kind to him and gave him food and drink and a pandanus leaf for his pillow.

predictions.source: “Niaa lua mea e fai I toku fenua?” ni ‘hili atu Tefolaha.
predictions.reference: “What are you doing on my island?” Tefolaha said.
predictions.prediction: "What are two things happening in my country?" asked Tefolaha.

predictions.source: “Niaa nei, heai he fakafetai,” munaa Pai. “Ni faite e maaua te fenua tenei!”
predictions.reference: “Wow, not even a thank you,” said Pai. “We made this island!”
predictions.prediction: "Now then, no thanks," said Pai. "We two found this land!"

predictions.source: “Ke fai he ‘tou fakataufaiga a po nei,” munaa Tefolaha. “Kae maua e koulua o taku mai toku igoa, aku ko fano. Kae fai e maua e aku o taku atu lua igoa, koulua ko olo.”
predictions.reference: “Let’s have a contest tonight,” Tefolaha said. “If you can guess my name, I’ll leave. But if I guess your names, you have to leave.”
predictions.prediction: "Let us have our competition tonight," said Tefolaha. "And if you two can tell me my name, I will go. But if I am able to tell you two names, you will go."

predictions.source: Pai mo Vau ni faka’tali ke fano Tefolaha keatea mo te fale, ni kata’kata iai a laaua. “Te Atua ni aumai e ia te tagata nei ke fai mo tupuga o tino Nanumea,” munaa Vau. “Ta ‘kau ki tana taahaoga ki te matemate igoa.”
predictions.reference: Pai and Vau waited for Tefolaha to leave the house, then smiled at each other. “God sent this man to be the ancestor of the people of Nanumea,” said Vau. “Let’s go along with his guessing game.”
predictions.prediction: Pai and Vau were waiting for Tefolaha to leave the house, and they were laughing about it. "God brought this man to be the ancestor of the people of Nanumea," said Vau. "Look at his readiness to face death by name."

predictions.source: Mai tua ifo i te po tena, Tefolaha ni kake ake ki luga i oka o te fale o Pai mo Vau. Ni iloa e laua me e noho a ia ikona, kae fai tele laa mea pelaa me he laa iloagina. Ni honi e ia te loko ki te kolokolo kae tukutuku ifo ke tautau mai luga o te piho o Pai. “Ou mata … Paiiii,” munaa Vau, mo te fakamoemoega ke lagona e Tefolaha te igoa. Oti una ni toe tukutuku ifo te loko ke pili ki te piho o Vau. “E tena hoki … Vauuuu,” munaa Pai.
predictions.reference: Later that day, Tefolaha climbed quietly up into the rafters of Pai and Vau’s house. They knew he was there, but pretended they didn’t. He tied a loko to a piece of string and lowered it until it was dangling close to Pai’s head. “Watch out … Paiiii,” said Vau, making sure Tefolaha heard the name. Then Tefolaha lowered the loko near to Vau’s head. “There it is again … Vauuuu,” said Pai.
predictions.prediction: From below that night, Tefolaha climbed up onto the roof of Pai and Vau's house. They both knew he was there, but they pretended as though they didn't know. He lowered a string down to dangle above Pai's navel. "My eyes … Paiiii," whispered Vau, hoping that Tefolaha would hear the name. Then he lowered the string again to touch Vau's navel. "That one too … Vauuuu," whispered Pai.

predictions.source: I te po tena, ni foki mai Tefolaha ki te fale. “Ko taa te taimi o te taahaoga matemate,” ana muna. “A koe ko Pai. A koe ko Vau. Koai toku igoa?”
predictions.reference: That night, Tefolaha came back to the hut. “It’s time for the guessing contest,” he said. “You’re Pai. You’re Vau. What’s my name?”
predictions.prediction: On that night, Tefolaha came back to the house. "It is the time of the deadly raid," he said. "You are Pai. You are Vau. What is my name?"

predictions.source: “E he iloa e maaua,” munaa Vau. “Koe pelaa koa hili.”
predictions.reference: “We don’t know,” said Vau. “Looks like you win.”
predictions.prediction: "We don't know," said Vau. "You might be right."

predictions.source: “Tausi la Nanumea,” munaa Pai. “E fakamanuiagina ou tino i kautama e uke.”
predictions.reference: “Take care of Nanumea,” said Pai. “It will bless your people for generations.”
predictions.prediction: "Take care of Nanumea," said Pai. "Your body is blessed with many talents."

predictions.source: Kae koi tuai a Pai mo Vau o olo, ni toe ‘ligi e laaua ni one fakataulaaitu ki te moana. “Meaalofa faopoopo moo tino o Nanumea,” munaa Pai. A one kona ni faite ake iai a motufo’liki ko Lafogaki mo Te Afua a Taepoa, kae tafahili atu, ko Lakena, tela i poo nei a tino Nanumea e `toki iai a lou talo.
predictions.reference: Just before Pai and Vau left, they poured more magical sand into the ocean. “Some more gifts for the people of Nanumea,” Pai said. The sand formed the islets of Lafogaki and Te Afua a Taepoa, and most important, Lakena, where today’s Nanumeans plant their taro gardens.
predictions.prediction: And still Pai and Vau went and scattered some sacred sand offerings into the sea. "Additional gifts for the people of Nanumea," said Pai. It was from that place that the small islands of Lafogaki and Te Afua a Taepoa arose, and further away, Lakena, where the people of Nanumea now harvest their taro leaves.
